parts.answer: preparing for fishing trips The general effect is the irony of military terms used both to criticise those who prepare carefully and describe their trip. • (engaged in a kind of ) military manoeuvre: involved, interested, take part in exercise/ advance by soldiers; careful planning by others is considered overdone by narrator • gear piled strategically: equipment organised according to need or likely event , suggests planning and forethought in case of emergency but piled suggests overdone, too much if it • eve of our campaign : night before they set out on military exercise/set out to battle; purpose • comrade: friend but suggests military , alongside them in battle • invested heavily: spent a great deal of money as if a business venture, suggest Royer would buy equipment irrespective of cost, jumping in enthusiastically • flashy state-of-the-art equipment: more style over substance, suggests Royer concerned with image and not necessarily what would be useful • bottom-of-the-range- soft-top sports car he’d borrowed: cheapest of its kind, low quality, not even his own car • unearthed a faded atlas: found old/out-of-date map, discovered, had been buried, not used for ages, forgotten, unclear

the canyon road The general effect is of an increasingly impassable road leading to an impressively deep canyon. • trickled down: reduced in size, suggests liquid movement, as if river – contrast • a ribbon of cement-hard dirt: road is thin, narrow, unforgiving • rutted and rough (on the tyres): hard grooved, unsuitable for their vehicle, could damage tyres • wormed (its way): the road bends around the trees, suggests a snake or creature moving close to the ground, unattractive and unsettling image of nature conspiring against them • slithering between the tall trees: (image) sliding from side to side, movement around trees, reminiscent of a venomous snake, peril • inching forwards (cautiously): taking particular care, moving forwards a little at a time, almost imperceptible as they are going so slowly, as if nearing the edge of something, afraid of falling • the trees fell away: come out of the forest suddenly, suggests the drop too • confronted by the magnificent pit: impressive canyon, though pit suggestive of a trap / hell • geological miracle: incredible natural feature, marvellous, cannot believe it has been formed out of rock, scale so amazing as if created by an act of a god • glittering like the tiniest trace of quicksilver: (image) seen from a distance river looks small, sparkling, mesmerising, precious; almost indistinguishable due to the depth of the canyon, like mercury, fluid, silver but only a trace, suggests fast moving and dangerous May/June 2018 Marking Criteria for Question 2 Table A, Reading: Language analysis: Use the following table to give a mark out of 10 for Reading. Band 1 9–10 Wide ranging and closely focused discussion of language with some high quality comments that add associations to words in both parts of the question, and demonstrate the writer’s reasons for using them. Tackles imagery with some precision and imagination. There is clear evidence that the candidate understands how language works. Band 2 7–8 Explanations are given of a

parts.text: 3(a) and (b) on this Question Paper. Question 3 Answer the questions in the order set. (a) Notes According to Passage B, why do people participate in fly fishing? Write your answer using short notes. Write one point per line. You do not need to use your own words. Up to 15 marks are available for the content of your answer. Why people
parts.answer: Reading content for Question 3(a) Give 1 mark per point listed below, up to a maximum of 15. Why people participate in fly fishing. Give 1 mark for a point about: 1 hooked / addicted / taken over their lives / occupies one’s life 2 romance of spending time in the water / romance of spending time by the water 3 improve social status / upward mobility / indication of wealth 4 business advantage(s) 5 beauty of setting(s) / remote spot(s) / exquisite place (s) / tranquil location 6 (excitement of) the rise / the spreading circle / excitement when fish start to feed 7 different skill levels / pecking order [allow any 2 examples: hard-core, serious, casual] 8 fun 9 different versions of flies / selecting the right type of fly [accept : ‘I knew the right fly’] 10 precision / challenge (of the sport) / technical skill(s) [allow example: cast (a full) 20 metres / fly just above (feeding) trout / land in precise spot / fly float(s) naturally] 11 takes a lifetime to master / learning to do it better / never stop improving 12 art / artistry / aesthetic(s) 13 theory / theories / theoretical 14 catch(ing) fish 15 anticipation of preparing (for it ) / anticipation of preparation 16 travel / trip(s) [allow example of going to Arctic and/or Tierra del Fuego] 17 studying the water / waiting for the flies to hatch 18 memories Notes: Although lifting of words and phrases from the passage is acceptable, candidates should show evidence of understanding and selection by clearly focusing on the key details. Over-lengthy lifting (e.g. of whole sections containing a number of points) should not be credited. Where errors of grammar / spelling seriously affect the accuracy of an idea, the point should not be awarded. May/June 2018 B Quality of Writing: concision, focus and use of own words Use the following table to give a mark out of 5 for Writing. Band 1 5 The response is well focused on the passage and the question. All points are expressed clearly, concisely and fluently, and in the candid
